title: How to Tell Thai Cooks You're Allergic to X (And Have Them Actually Believe You)

vocab.explanation: แพ้ means 'to be allergic to.' It goes between the subject and the allergen: หนูแพ้กุ้ง is 'I'm allergic to shrimp.' One thing worth knowing: แพ้ also means 'to lose' in Thai — context always makes the food meaning clear. Land on a falling tone when you say it: phâae.

exampleSentences.thai: ผมแพ้นมวัวครับ
exampleSentences.note: ผม is the polite first-person pronoun for men. Same subject-verb-object structure every time.

vocab.explanation: กุ้ง means shrimp or prawns — Thai doesn't distinguish between the two. This is one of the most critical allergy words because shrimp appears in pad thai, fried rice, many curries, and dishes that look entirely vegetarian. If you have a shrimp allergy, กุ้ง is the first word you need.

exampleSentences.thai: ร้านนี้มีกุ้งผัดพริกไหมคะ
exampleSentences.translit: ráan níi mii gûng phàt-phrík mǎi khá
exampleSentences.english: Does this restaurant have stir-fried shrimp with chilli?
exampleSentences.note: กุ้ง used in a menu context — same word, different situation from the allergy conversation.

vocab.thaiSplit: อาหาร ทะเล
vocab.explanation: อาหารทะเล literally means 'sea food' — อาหาร is 'food' and ทะเล is 'sea.' Use this when you're allergic to all seafood, not just one thing. If you only say กุ้ง, the cook might remove the shrimp but keep the squid or crab. อาหารทะเล covers everything from the ocean.

exampleSentences.thai: หนูแพ้อาหารทะเลทุกชนิดค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: nǔu phâae aa-hǎan thá-lee thúk chá-nít khâ
exampleSentences.english: I'm allergic to all types of seafood.
exampleSentences.note: ทุกชนิด means 'every kind' — adding this makes the restriction even broader and clearer.

vocab.explanation: ถั่ว covers nuts, peanuts, and legumes. Peanut allergies are especially important to communicate in Thailand because peanuts appear in pad thai, satay sauce, some salads, and many curries. For peanuts specifically you can say ถั่วลิสง — thùa-lí-sǒng — but just ถั่ว gets the message across in most situations. It's a falling tone: thùa.

exampleSentences.thai: แกงนี้ใส่ถั่วลิสงไหมคะ
exampleSentences.translit: gaeng níi sài thùa-lí-sǒng mǎi khá
exampleSentences.english: Does this curry have peanuts in it?
exampleSentences.note: ใส่ means 'put in' or 'add' — very useful for asking about specific ingredients.

vocab.thaiSplit: กิน ไม่ได้
vocab.explanation: กินไม่ได้ means 'cannot eat.' กิน is 'to eat,' ไม่ is 'not,' and ได้ means 'able to.' So literally: 'eat not able.' This phrase signals a real restriction rather than a preference. Paired with แพ้ and เลย at the end — กินไม่ได้เลย — it becomes very emphatic: 'I really cannot eat it at all.'

exampleSentences.thai: แป้งสาลีกินไม่ได้เลยครับ
exampleSentences.translit: pâeng-sǎa-lii gin mâi dâai looei khráp
exampleSentences.english: I absolutely cannot eat wheat flour.
exampleSentences.note: เลย at the end means 'at all' and adds strong emphasis. Useful for gluten allergies — แป้งสาลี is wheat flour.

vocab.thaiSplit: น้ำมัน หอย
vocab.explanation: น้ำมันหอย is oyster sauce — น้ำมัน means 'oil' and หอย means 'shellfish.' This is a hidden allergen in Thai cooking: oyster sauce goes into stir-fried vegetables, noodle dishes, and many things that look like they have nothing to do with seafood. If you have a shellfish allergy and only say 'no seafood,' the oyster sauce will almost certainly still be in your food. Ask about it separately.

exampleSentences.thai: ขอไม่ใส่น้ำมันหอยได้ไหมคะ
exampleSentences.translit: khǎaw mâi sài nám-man hǎawy dâai mǎi khá
exampleSentences.english: Can you leave out the oyster sauce?
exampleSentences.note: ขอ is 'I'd like' or 'may I.' ไม่ใส่ is 'not add.' A clean, polite request any Thai cook will understand.

vocab.thaiSplit: มี ส่วนผสม
vocab.explanation: มีส่วนผสม means 'contains' or 'has as an ingredient.' มี is 'have' and ส่วนผสม means 'ingredients.' You use it to ask whether a dish contains something: อาหารนี้มีส่วนผสมของกุ้งไหม — 'Does this food contain shrimp?' The word ของ after it means 'of,' and ไหม at the end turns it into a yes/no question.

exampleSentences.thai: อาหารนี้มีส่วนผสมของถั่วไหมคะ
exampleSentences.translit: aa-hǎan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng thùa mǎi khá
exampleSentences.english: Does this food contain any nuts?
exampleSentences.note: Without ไหม it's a statement; adding ไหม at the end makes it a question — no word-order change needed.

vocab.explanation: อันตราย means 'dangerous' — and it's your secret weapon. Vendors sometimes assume foreigners say 'allergic' when they just mean 'I'd rather not have it.' But อันตราย signals a real medical situation. กินแล้วอันตราย means 'eating it is dangerous,' and that phrase changes the entire conversation. Thai people take safety seriously, and this word gets the right response.

exampleSentences.thai: ถ้าหนูกินกุ้งอันตรายมากเลยค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: thâa nǔu gin gûng an-ta-raai mâak looei khâ
exampleSentences.english: If I eat shrimp it is very dangerous.
exampleSentences.note: ถ้า is 'if' — the conditional 'if I eat X, dangerous' is clear, direct, and effective for communicating medical need.

blocks.speakerNote: A = tourist (Nine: hair up, backpack strap visible, slightly careful delivery). B = Thai street food vendor (Nine: apron, relaxed and warm, natural Thai speed). Visual cue: lighting shift — warm overhead lantern for B, cooler side light for A. Voice modulation: A speaks more slowly and carefully, B speaks at full natural pace. Recording note: This block is the opening clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short.

lines.english: At a street food stall somewhere in Thailand...
lines.speaker: narrator

lines.thai: ขอโทษนะคะ อยากถามหน่อยค่ะ
lines.translit: khǎaw thôot ná khá, yàak thǎam nàawy khâ
lines.english: Excuse me, can I ask you something?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ค่ะ สั่งอะไรดีคะ
lines.translit: khâ, sàng a-rai dii khá
lines.english: Sure! What would you like to order?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: หนูแพ้กุ้งค่ะ
lines.translit: nǔu phâae gûng khâ
lines.english: I'm allergic to shrimp.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: แพ้จริงเหรอคะ หรือแค่ไม่ชอบ
lines.translit: phâae jing rǒo khá, rǔu khâae mâi châawp
lines.english: Actually allergic? Or just don't like it?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: แพ้จริงค่ะ กินแล้วอันตรายมากเลยค่ะ
lines.translit: phâae jing khâ, gin láaew an-ta-raai mâak looei khâ
lines.english: Really allergic. Eating it is very dangerous.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: โอ้โห งั้นเมนูนี้กินไม่ได้นะคะ มีกุ้งอยู่
lines.translit: ôo-hǒo, ngán mee-nuu níi gin mâi dâai ná khá, mii gûng yùu
lines.english: Oh wow, then you can't eat this dish — it has shrimp.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: แล้วมีเมนูไหนที่ไม่มีกุ้งบ้างคะ
lines.translit: láaew mii mee-nuu nǎi thîi mâi mii gûng bâang khá
lines.english: Is there anything on the menu without shrimp?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: มีค่ะ ผัดผักรวมไม่มีกุ้งเลยค่ะ
lines.translit: mii khâ, phàt-phàk-ruam mâi mii gûng looei khâ
lines.english: Yes! Stir-fried mixed vegetables — no shrimp at all.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ขอบคุณมากเลยค่ะ ช่วยชีวิตหนูเลย
lines.translit: khàawp-khun mâak looei khâ, chûuay chii-wít nǔu looei
lines.english: Thank you so much! You literally saved my life.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ยินดีค่ะ
lines.translit: yin-dii khâ
lines.english: You're welcome!
lines.speaker: B

blocks.speakerNote:
Default opener: the oyster-sauce hidden-allergen fact. Three alternatives below.

Alternative 1 (pattern from sketch): 'Did you catch that question the vendor asked? แพ้จริงเหรอ — really allergic? She wasn't being rude. That's a completely normal response in Thailand. Vendors hear foreigners say they'd rather not have something all the time. The word that changes everything is อันตราย — dangerous. I'm going to teach you that word today, along with seven others.'

Alternative 2 (direct contrast): 'In English, saying you're allergic usually ends the conversation. In a busy Thai kitchen, it sometimes starts a negotiation. The one word that shuts that down is อันตราย — dangerous. Add it and the tone of the whole exchange changes. That's what today is about.'

Alternative 3 (statistic): 'Thailand welcomes over 35 million international visitors a year, and food-related allergic reactions are one of the most common medical complaints among tourists. Not because Thai food is dangerous — but because the communication breaks down. Today we fix that.'

lines.english: That sketch? She was telling the vendor she's allergic to shrimp — and by the end of this video, you'll be able to say every single line of that conversation yourself.

Here's something most people don't realise about Thai cooking: น้ำมันหอย — oyster sauce — goes into stir-fried vegetables, noodle dishes, even some soups. It's used like a base seasoning, almost invisible. So if you have a shellfish allergy and you only say 'no shrimp,' the shrimp disappears — but the oyster sauce stays. Knowing the right words doesn't just help you order. It keeps you safe.

Today I'm going to teach you eight phrases for communicating food allergies in Thai. We'll go through each word one by one, put them together in a real conversation, break it down sentence by sentence, then practise together. By the end, you'll be able to name your allergy, ask whether a dish contains it, and make clear that this is a medical need — not just a preference. All in Thai.
Let's start with the vocabulary. I'm going to go through each word one by one. For each one, I'll say the Thai, explain what it means, and show you how to use it in a sentence. Ready? Let's go.

lines.thai: แพ้
lines.translit: phâae

lines.english: Our first word — and honestly the most important one.

แพ้ — phâae. This is the word for 'to be allergic to.' หนูแพ้กุ้ง means 'I'm allergic to shrimp.' It's a verb — subject, then แพ้, then what you're allergic to. One interesting thing: แพ้ also means 'to lose' in Thai, like losing a game or a match. But in a food context, everyone knows exactly what you mean. When you say it, the tone falls: phâae. That falling tone is what makes it land correctly.

lines.thai: ผมแพ้นมวัวครับ
lines.translit: phǒm phâae nom wua khráp

lines.english: 'I'm allergic to cow's milk.' ผม is 'I' for men — women use หนู. Then แพ้, then the allergen. Same pattern every single time.
OK, next word — a very common allergen in Thai food.

lines.thai: กุ้ง
lines.translit: gûng

lines.english: กุ้ง — gûng. Shrimp, or prawns — Thai doesn't distinguish between the two. This word is critical because shrimp is absolutely everywhere in Thai cooking: pad thai, fried rice, many curries, and even some dishes that look entirely vegetarian have shrimp paste or dried shrimp worked into the base. If shrimp is your allergen, this is the first word you need to know cold.

lines.thai: ร้านนี้มีกุ้งผัดพริกไหมคะ
lines.translit: ráan níi mii gûng phàt-phrík mǎi khá

lines.english: 'Does this restaurant have stir-fried shrimp with chilli?' — that's กุ้ง in a menu context rather than an allergy context, but once you know the word, you'll hear it constantly.
This next one covers all seafood — not just shrimp.

lines.thai: อาหารทะเล
lines.translit: aa-hǎan thá-lee

lines.english: อาหารทะเล — aa-hǎan thá-lee. Seafood. Literally 'sea food' — อาหาร is 'food,' ทะเล is 'sea.' Use this when you're allergic to all seafood, not just one specific ingredient. If you only say กุ้ง, the cook might remove the shrimp and keep the squid and crab. อาหารทะเล tells them to avoid everything from the ocean, and that's especially important in coastal regions where seafood is in almost every dish.

lines.thai: หนูแพ้อาหารทะเลทุกชนิดค่ะ
lines.translit: nǔu phâae aa-hǎan thá-lee thúk chá-nít khâ

lines.english: 'I'm allergic to all types of seafood.' ทุกชนิด means 'every kind' — if you want to be thorough, add that at the end and it covers absolutely everything.
Next — another very common allergy, and a tricky one in Thailand.

lines.thai: ถั่ว
lines.translit: thùa

lines.english: ถั่ว — thùa. Nuts, peanuts, beans — it covers all of them. Peanut allergies are especially important to communicate clearly in Thailand because peanuts show up in pad thai, satay sauce, some salads, and various curries. For peanuts specifically you can say ถั่วลิสง — thùa-lí-sǒng — but just saying ถั่ว gets the message across in most contexts. Notice the tone: thùa — it falls.

lines.thai: แกงนี้ใส่ถั่วลิสงไหมคะ
lines.translit: gaeng níi sài thùa-lí-sǒng mǎi khá

lines.english: 'Does this curry have peanuts in it?' — ใส่ means 'put in' or 'add.' Very useful when asking about specific ingredients.
This next one is a phrase, but you'll use it constantly.

lines.thai: กินไม่ได้
lines.translit: gin mâi dâai

lines.english: กินไม่ได้ — gin mâi dâai. 'Cannot eat.' กิน is 'to eat,' ไม่ is 'not,' and ได้ means 'able to.' So literally: 'eat not able.' This phrase signals a real restriction, not just a dislike. When you pair it with แพ้ and add เลย at the end — กินไม่ได้เลย — it becomes very emphatic: 'I really cannot eat it at all.' เลย is the word that adds that strong, serious tone.

lines.thai: แป้งสาลีกินไม่ได้เลยครับ
lines.translit: pâeng-sǎa-lii gin mâi dâai looei khráp

lines.english: 'I absolutely cannot eat wheat flour.' แป้งสาลี is wheat flour — useful if you have a gluten allergy. And เลย at the end makes it emphatic: 'at all,' 'whatsoever.'
This one surprises a lot of people — it's a hidden allergen.

lines.thai: น้ำมันหอย
lines.translit: nám-man hǎawy

lines.english: น้ำมันหอย — nám-man hǎawy. Oyster sauce. น้ำมัน means 'oil' and หอย means 'shellfish' or 'oyster.' This is one of the most important hidden allergens in Thai cooking: oyster sauce goes into stir-fried vegetables, noodle dishes, and many things that have no visible seafood at all. If you say 'no seafood' but don't mention น้ำมันหอย specifically, it will almost certainly still be in your food. This word is worth memorising on its own.

lines.thai: ขอไม่ใส่น้ำมันหอยได้ไหมคะ
lines.translit: khǎaw mâi sài nám-man hǎawy dâai mǎi khá

lines.english: 'Can you leave out the oyster sauce?' — ขอ is 'I'd like' or 'may I,' ไม่ใส่ is 'not add.' A polite, clear request any Thai cook will understand immediately.
This next phrase is how you ask whether an ingredient is in a dish.

lines.thai: มีส่วนผสม
lines.translit: mii sùan phà-sǒm

lines.english: มีส่วนผสม — mii sùan phà-sǒm. 'Contains' or 'has as an ingredient.' มี is 'have' or 'there is,' and ส่วนผสม means 'ingredients.' You use it to ask: อาหารนี้มีส่วนผสมของกุ้งไหม — 'Does this food contain shrimp?' The ของ after it means 'of,' so you're asking whether the ingredients include that thing, and ไหม at the very end turns the whole sentence into a yes/no question.

lines.thai: อาหารนี้มีส่วนผสมของถั่วไหมคะ
lines.translit: aa-hǎan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng thùa mǎi khá

lines.english: 'Does this food contain any nuts?' — same pattern: this food, contains ingredients of, nuts, question marker. Once you have this structure, you can swap in any allergen.
And our last word — this is your secret weapon.

lines.thai: อันตราย
lines.translit: an-ta-raai

lines.english: อันตราย — an-ta-raai. Dangerous. I cannot stress how useful this word is. Street food vendors and restaurant staff sometimes assume foreigners say 'allergic' when they really just mean 'I'd prefer not to have it.' But อันตราย — dangerous — signals that this is a real medical situation. กินแล้วอันตราย means 'eating it is dangerous,' and that phrase changes the entire conversation. Thai people take safety very seriously, and this word gets the right response every time.

lines.thai: ถ้าหนูกินกุ้งอันตรายมากเลยค่ะ
lines.translit: thâa nǔu gin gûng an-ta-raai mâak looei khâ

lines.english: 'If I eat shrimp it is very dangerous.' ถ้า is 'if' — so you're building a clear conditional: 'if I eat X, dangerous.' Direct, serious, and effective.

OK, so now you know all eight words. What I'm going to do now is put them together into a short conversation — imagine you're sitting down at a Thai restaurant and you need to tell them about your shrimp allergy. I'm going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Don't pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can catch. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: ขอโทษนะคะ หนูแพ้กุ้งค่ะ
แพ้จริงๆ เลยค่ะ กินแล้วอันตรายมากเลย
อาหารจานนี้มีส่วนผสมของกุ้งไหมคะ
ถ้ามีกุ้ง หนูกินไม่ได้เลยนะคะ
มีเมนูไหนที่ไม่มีอาหารทะเลบ้างคะ

lines.english: How was that? Did you catch any of those phrases? Don't worry if you didn't get everything — that's completely normal. Now let's break it down.
I'm going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I'll say the Thai, and then I'll explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: ขอโทษนะคะ หนูแพ้กุ้งค่ะ
lines.translit: khǎaw thôot ná khá, nǔu phâae gûng khâ
khǎaw thôot ná khá, nǔu phâae gûng khâ

lines.english: Excuse me — I'm allergic to shrimp.

ขอโทษนะคะ is your polite opener — 'excuse me.' Then หนูแพ้กุ้ง: subject, verb, object. หนู is 'I' — a humble, softened first person used by women. ล Men use ผม. Notice the word order: subject, แพ้, then what you're allergic to — that's the same as English, which makes this structure easy to remember and adapt.

lines.thai: แพ้จริงๆ เลยค่ะ กินแล้วอันตรายมากเลย
lines.translit: phâae jing-jing looei khâ, gin láaew an-ta-raai mâak looei
phâae jing-jing looei khâ, gin láaew an-ta-raai mâak looei

lines.english: I'm really truly allergic. Eating it is very dangerous.

จริงๆ means 'really' or 'truly' — repeating จริง doubles the emphasis. เลย is an intensifier meaning 'indeed' or 'really.' Then กินแล้วอันตรายมาก: กิน is 'eat,' แล้ว connects the action to the result, อันตราย is 'dangerous,' มาก is 'very.' In English we'd say 'eating it is very dangerous' — but Thai puts the degree word มาก after the adjective, at the end of the phrase, rather than before it.

lines.thai: อาหารจานนี้มีส่วนผสมของกุ้งไหมคะ
lines.translit: aa-hǎan jaan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng gûng mǎi khá
aa-hǎan jaan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng gûng mǎi khá

lines.english: Does this dish contain shrimp?

อาหารจานนี้ — 'this dish.' จาน is 'plate' or 'dish,' นี้ is 'this.' มีส่วนผสมของกุ้ง — 'contains shrimp.' Then ไหมคะ at the end is the yes/no question marker. This is a key pattern: in Thai, you don't change the word order to form a question the way English does. You keep the same sentence and just add ไหม at the end. 'This dish contains shrimp' and 'Does this dish contain shrimp?' are word-for-word identical except for ไหม.

lines.thai: ถ้ามีกุ้ง หนูกินไม่ได้เลยนะคะ
lines.translit: thâa mii gûng, nǔu gin mâi dâai looei ná khá
thâa mii gûng, nǔu gin mâi dâai looei ná khá

lines.english: If it has shrimp, I really can't eat it.

ถ้า means 'if' — it goes at the start of the condition, just like English. ถ้ามีกุ้ง: 'if there is shrimp.' Then หนูกินไม่ได้เลย: 'I absolutely cannot eat it.' Notice นะคะ at the very end — that's a softening particle. It keeps the message polite and warm even though the content is firm. Thai people use นะ constantly to take the edge off a direct statement without weakening it.

lines.thai: มีเมนูไหนที่ไม่มีอาหารทะเลบ้างคะ
lines.translit: mii mee-nuu nǎi thîi mâi mii aa-hǎan thá-lee bâang khá
mii mee-nuu nǎi thîi mâi mii aa-hǎan thá-lee bâang khá

lines.english: Is there anything on the menu without seafood?

มีเมนูไหน — 'is there any menu item.' ไหน means 'which' or 'any.' Then ที่ไม่มีอาหารทะเล is a relative clause: ที่ is the relative marker — like 'that' or 'which' in English — ไม่มี is 'doesn't have,' อาหารทะเล is seafood. So: 'Is there a menu item that doesn't have seafood?' บ้าง at the end softens the question and gives it the nuance of 'at all' or 'any.' Drop บ้าง and the sentence is grammatical but sounds blunter.

Now it's your turn to practise. I'm going to say each sentence, and I want you to repeat after me. Say it out loud — don't just think it in your head! I'll leave a gap after each one. Speaking is how you make it stick.

lines.thai: ขอโทษนะคะ หนูแพ้กุ้งค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ขอโทษ นะ คะ หนู แพ้ กุ้ง ค่ะ
lines.translit: khǎaw thôot ná khá nǔu phâae gûng khâ

lines.thai: แพ้จริงๆ เลยค่ะ กินแล้วอันตรายมากเลย
lines.thaiSplit: แพ้ จริงๆ เลย ค่ะ กิน แล้ว อันตราย มาก เลย
lines.translit: phâae jing-jing looei khâ gin láaew an-ta-raai mâak looei

lines.thai: อาหารจานนี้มีส่วนผสมของกุ้งไหมคะ
lines.thaiSplit: อาหาร จาน นี้ มี ส่วนผสม ของ กุ้ง ไหม คะ
lines.translit: aa-hǎan jaan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng gûng mǎi khá

lines.thai: ถ้ามีกุ้ง หนูกินไม่ได้เลยนะคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ถ้า มี กุ้ง หนู กิน ไม่ได้ เลย นะ คะ
lines.translit: thâa mii gûng nǔu gin mâi dâai looei ná khá

lines.thai: มีเมนูไหนที่ไม่มีอาหารทะเลบ้างคะ
lines.thaiSplit: มี เมนู ไหน ที่ ไม่มี อาหาร ทะเล บ้าง คะ
lines.translit: mii mee-nuu nǎi thîi mâi mii aa-hǎan thá-lee bâang khá

lines.english: Great job! That last sentence is a long one — if it felt hard, rewind and do it again. Repetition is exactly how you get comfortable with longer phrases.
OK, quiz time! I'm going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I'll give you a few seconds to think, then I'll give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Hold a 3-second pause after 'Try saying it now...' before cutting to the answer block.

lines.english: You're at a restaurant and you want to tell the server you're allergic to peanuts. How do you say that?
Try saying it now...

blocks.speakerNote: Smile, say it clearly, then explain.

lines.thai: หนูแพ้ถั่วค่ะ
lines.translit: nǔu phâae thùa khâ

lines.english: That's right — หนูแพ้ถั่ว means 'I'm allergic to nuts.' Same structure as always: subject, แพ้, allergen. Clean and simple.

blocks.speakerNote: Pause 3 seconds after the prompt line.

lines.english: A dish arrives and you're not sure if it contains seafood. How do you ask if this dish contains seafood?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: อาหารจานนี้มีส่วนผสมของอาหารทะเลไหมคะ
lines.translit: aa-hǎan jaan níi mii sùan phà-sǒm khǎawng aa-hǎan thá-lee mǎi khá

lines.english: That's right — อาหารจานนี้มีส่วนผสมของอาหารทะเลไหม: 'Does this dish contain seafood?' This dish, contains ingredients of, seafood, question marker. You've got the full pattern.

blocks.speakerNote: Pause 3 seconds after the prompt line.

lines.english: You need to make clear that your allergy is a real medical issue — not just a preference. How do you say 'eating it is very dangerous'?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: กินแล้วอันตรายมากค่ะ
lines.translit: gin láaew an-ta-raai mâak khâ

lines.english: That's right — กินแล้วอันตรายมาก means 'eating it is very dangerous.' Remember อันตราย — that's the word that makes Thai cooks take your allergy seriously.
Before you go — let's run through all eight words one more time.

Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today. แพ้ — allergic to. กุ้ง — shrimp. อาหารทะเล — seafood. ถั่ว — nuts. กินไม่ได้ — cannot eat. น้ำมันหอย — oyster sauce. มีส่วนผสม — contains. And อันตราย — dangerous. Eight words that can make a real difference.

Thanks for watching! If this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one. And if you have a specific allergy word you want to know in Thai, drop it in the comments — I read every single one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates. Next episode is about tasting and recommending food — a natural follow-on from ordering safely.

lines.english: Next time — once you know what's safe to eat, you'll want to know how to say 'give it a try.' Here's a phrase to get you started:

lines.thai: ลองชิมดูนะคะ
lines.translit: lawng chim duu ná khá
